items.q: How does a catalyst speed up a reaction?
items.correct: It provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy.
items.misconception: A catalyst lowers the activation energy of the original reaction pathway.
items.random_wrong: A catalyst gives extra kinetic energy to the reactant particles.
items.why: Textbook diagrams often show 'Ea lowered' without making clear it's a new pathway, leading students to think catalysts modify the original pathway.

items.q: At room temperature and pressure, what volume does 1 mole of any gas occupy?
items.correct: Approximately 24 dm^3 (24,000 cm^3), regardless of the identity of the gas.
items.misconception: Heavier gases occupy a smaller volume per mole because their particles are denser.
items.random_wrong: Different gases occupy completely different volumes proportional to their molar mass.
items.why: Students assume heavier particles must be more compact, missing that gas volume depends only on number of particles, T and P (Avogadro's law).

items.q: Why is the 4s orbital filled before the 3d orbital?
items.correct: Because the 4s orbital has a lower energy than 3d when both are empty, so electrons fill it first.
items.misconception: Because the 4s shell is closer to the nucleus than the 3d shell.
items.random_wrong: Because d orbitals can only fill after p orbitals in the next shell.
items.why: Students assume shell number directly correlates with distance/energy ordering, so 3-anything must come before 4-anything — but energy ordering crosses over at 4s/3d.
The answer examiners want is: chromatography. “filtration” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's move freely in all directions. “vibrate in fixed positions” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Pure water melts at exactly what temperature (degrees C)?
items.why: Exact answer: 0. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
